title_en: The Met Turns Orientalism Inside Out
title_zh: 大都会艺术博物馆将“东方主义”翻了个个

paragraphs.en: In a new show, exotic colonial fantasies are set beside paintings that depict the so-called East from within.
paragraphs.zh: 在一个新展览中，异国殖民式的幻想被放在与那些描绘所谓“东方”内部视角的绘画并列的位置。

paragraphs.en: Few great writers have been as brainlessly happy as Gustave Flaubert in Egypt. From the moment he arrived, in the autumn of 1849, every detail pinched him with exotic perfection. The color of the earth reminded him of Nubian flesh, and the water looked like melted silver. He smoked long pipes while sprawled out on divans, wearing a red tarboosh. He slept with courtesans and dancers, and went to a bathhouse hoping to “skewer” a young boy. He also couldn’t get enough of the camels, the way they moved like turkeys and had necks like swans, and tired himself out trying to imitate their gargling noises. “I have found, clearly delineated, everything that was hazy in my mind,” he wrote. “Facts have taken the place of suppositions—so excellently so that it is often as though I were suddenly coming upon old forgotten dreams.”
paragraphs.zh: 他躺在长沙发上抽着长烟斗，头戴红色塔拉布什式软帽。他与娼妓和舞女同寝，去浴场希望“钩上”一个年轻男孩。他也对骆驼欲罢不能，既惊叹它们像火鸡一样的动作、又像天鹅般的脖子，还使劲想模仿它们咕噜的叫声，自己累得不行。“我已经清楚地看见了，我脑中那些模糊不清的事物，”他写道。“事实取代了猜测——好到让我常常觉得仿佛忽然闯入了早已遗忘的梦境。”

paragraphs.en: Flaubert’s case is one of many that Edward Said cites in “Orientalism,” his catalytic book from 1978. Without being too glib, one could summarize Said’s achievement as the diagnosis of a mass delusion hiding in plain sight. Orientalism—the study of the cultures and customs of the so-called East by the so-called West—compulsively rehearsed myths about the dark, violent, sensuous hordes of Asia and the Islamic Mediterranean. Said wasn’t the first to notice this, but he showed how scholars and writers had turned their own ignorance into an art form, creating an elaborate fiction—“the Orient”—to reduce vastly different cultures into an abstract, timeless unity that Europe and America could define themselves against and then feel good about dominating.
paragraphs.zh: 《东方主义》一书中爱德华·萨义德列举的案例之一，就是福楼拜的经历。若不说得过分轻率，人们可以把萨义德的成就概括为揭露了一个明摆着却不被看见的“群体性错觉”。“东方主义”——“西方”对“东方”文化与习俗的研究——不断反复演练着关于亚洲及伊斯兰地中海世界黑暗、暴力、感官化人群的神话。萨义德并非第一个注意到这一点的人，但他展示了学者和作家如何把自己的无知升华为一种艺术形式，制造出精巧的虚构——“东方”——把截然不同的文化压缩成一个抽象的、超越时间的整体，于是欧洲和美国就能以之为对象来定义自身，并在由此建立的支配关系中获得心安。

paragraphs.en: “Orientalism: Between Fact and Fantasy” is the first exhibition at the Met to tackle the subject, rounding up paintings, drawings, ceramics, photographs, textiles, and a few guns and helmets. Surprisingly, it’s also the first major collaboration between the departments of European Paintings and Islamic Art. The show reads as a curious experiment in intra-museum diplomacy, tucked into four modest rooms that bridge the Islamic and European galleries. The focus is on the nineteenth century, when most of the world’s land fell under colonial rule, and when artistic styles cycled between East and West. Orientalism, the show suggests, wasn’t a one-way street but a traffic in misappropriation.
paragraphs.zh: 《东方主义：事实与幻想之间》是大都会博物馆首次以该主题出展，展出范围涵盖绘画、素描、陶瓷、摄影、纺织品，以及少量枪支和头盔。令人惊讶的是，这也是欧洲绘画部与伊斯兰艺术部之间第一次大型合作。展览像一次有趣的馆内外交试验，被安放在连接伊斯兰艺术馆和欧洲艺术馆的四个不大的展厅中。展览聚焦19世纪——当时世界上大部分陆地都处于殖民统治之下，也是艺术风格在东西方间往返流动的时期。展览所示，所谓“东方主义”并非单向通道，而是一种挪用与误取的“车流”。

paragraphs.en: The start of the exhibition is a bit jumbled. One section tracks the influence of the Alhambra complex, in Granada, where artists trekked to whet their appetites for the exotic. Another section gathers various candlesticks, mosque lamps, and bowls to show how European and American craftsmen cribbed Islamic designs to reform the ugliness of mass-produced goods in the West. The show sparks to life once it strikes two artists together: Jean-Léon Gérôme and Osman Hamdi Bey. There are other highlights—paintings by Géricault and Alexandre-Gabriel Decamps, a few Persian swan-neck bottles and their Tiffany glass imitations—but the gamble here is to play Gérôme and Hamdi off each other, the outsider and the insider. One was a Frenchman who dreamed of the Orient and made frequent trips to modern-day Turkey and Egypt (and, like Flaubert, developed a thing for camels); the other was a diplomat-painter who was born in Constantinople and educated in Paris, and then returned home to paint his world from within. Gérôme’s “The Snake Charmer” (ca. 1879) is the poster child of Orientalism and was put on the cover of Said’s book. Hamdi broke the auction record for a Turkish painting when “The Tortoise Trainer” (1906) sold in 2004, for roughly $3.5 million.
paragraphs.zh: 展览开场有些杂乱。一个版块追踪了格拉纳达阿尔罕布拉宫的影响，艺术家们曾远赴那里以刺激对“异域”的想象与欲望。另一个版块汇集了各式烛台、清真寺台灯和碗碟，展示欧洲和美国工匠如何借用伊斯兰设计，来改造西方那些量产品的“难看”面貌。展览真正亮起来，是因为它同时并置了两位艺术家：让-莱昂·热罗姆（Jean-Léon Gérôme）和奥斯曼·哈姆迪·贝伊（Osman Hamdi Bey）。除此之外还有其他亮点——格里考与亚历山大-加布里埃尔·德卡姆的油画、几只波斯天鹅颈瓶及其蒂凡尼玻璃仿制品——但这次策展的“赌注”是拿热罗姆与哈姆迪对照观看：外来者与内行者之间的对照。一个是法国人，梦想着“东方”，并频繁前往今土耳其与埃及（且像福楼拜一样迷恋骆驼）；另一个则是生于君士坦丁堡、在巴黎受过教育的外交官兼画家，后来回到家乡，从内部描绘自己的世界。热罗姆的《吸蛇者》（约1879年）是“东方主义”的代表作，也被选为赛义德著作的封面。哈姆迪在2004年《乌龟驯兽师》（1906）拍卖时打破了土耳其油画纪录，成交价约350万美元。

paragraphs.en: Gérôme is the rare artist who’s easy to like and to hate. Every painting is a model of academic crispness and polish, filled with visual cues and prearranged emotional payoffs. “The Snake Charmer,” on loan from the Clark, in Williamstown, Massachusetts, shows a naked boy wrapped in an enormous snake while an old man plays a flute. We’re brought into the scene via the boy’s illuminated buttocks, and conducted to the background, where a crew of ten soldiers gawk at the spectacle. To the right, there’s a guy with a beatific smile who seems to be enjoying more than the snake, an enormous gun propped vertically between his legs. Tabulate the number of stiff objects in the painting (gun, stick, flute, sabre, etc.) and you’ll see that the whole thing is a festival of phallic abundance. Just in case you forget to feel aroused, Gérôme reminds you to.
paragraphs.zh: 热罗姆是一位“既容易被人喜欢又容易被人憎恨”的罕见艺术家。每一幅画都像学术风格的范本，精确而光洁，充满了视觉线索和预设的情绪回报。《吸蛇者》借展自马萨诸塞州威廉斯敦的克拉克博物馆，画面里一个赤裸的男孩被一条巨蛇缠住，老人边吹长笛边表演。观众被引入画面，是从男孩被灯光照亮的臀部开始，随后被带向后方：一队十名士兵正呆呆注视着这一景象。右侧有一位脸带幸福笑容的男子，似乎享受的远不止这条蛇；他双腿之间竖着一把巨大的枪。若把画中的僵硬物件逐一列数（枪、棍、长笛、军刀等），你会发现整幅画其实是一场“男性性征”过度丰沛的狂欢。即使你差点把这种兴奋感遗忘了，热罗姆会立刻让你想起。

paragraphs.en: The snake charmer is, in effect, a stand-in for Gérôme, whose art relies on seduction and deception. Whether it’s a steamy bathhouse nude or a mosque scene, his paintings follow the cardinal rule of the Orientalist handbook, which is to use a mashup of decorative styles to yield a tantalizing essence. In “The Snake Charmer,” the Listerine-colored tiles in the background are inspired by Istanbul’s Topkapi Palace, the stone floor is drawn from architecture in Cairo, and the assorted costumes are from different times and places—an Ottoman helmet worn by a man on the left, for instance, is from the fifteenth century and personally belonged to Gérôme. (It’s in a display case here, next to a first edition of Said’s book.) As an establishment artist who taught at the École des Beaux-Arts and denounced the Impressionists, Gérôme has always been an easy target for serious artists and critics, who like to trash his “lightweight style” and his crowd-pleasing subjects. Émile Zola icily commented on Gérôme, “Here the subject matter is everything; the painting is nothing.”
paragraphs.zh: 这位“吸蛇者”在某种意义上是热罗姆本人的替身，而他的艺术恰恰建立在诱惑和欺骗之上。无论是蒸汽弥漫的浴室裸像，还是清真寺场景，他的画都遵循东方主义手册的一条基本法则：把多种装饰性风格拼接在一起，以提炼出一种令人遐想的“精髓”。在《吸蛇者》中，背景里的瓷砖是按伊斯坦布尔托普卡普宫的色彩搭配来的，石地板取自开罗建筑，而服饰则来自不同的时代与地域——例如左侧那位男子戴的奥斯曼头盔，来自15世纪，且据说是热罗姆本人拥有的实物。（它目前与赛义德著作的初版一起陈列在展柜中。）作为当时的官方派画家，热罗姆在凡尔赛高等美术学院任教并公开批评印象派，因此一直是严肃艺术家和评论家轻易攻击的对象；他们习惯性地抨击他的“轻佻风格”和迎合大众的题材。艾米尔·左拉曾冷冷地评了他一句：‘在他那里，题材就是一切；画本身什么都不是。’

paragraphs.en: That’s a serious miscalculation, in my opinion. How do we account for the beauty of “Bashi-Bazouk” (1868-69)? The subject is just a man with a colorful headdress. Bashi-bazouks were a feared paramilitary outfit in the Ottoman Army, known for cracking skulls, and yet the sitter is a paragon of calm, not violence. He’s almost certainly a civilian whom Gérôme put in a costume. There’s a rifle on his shoulder, but it’s not shooting anytime soon; if anything, the mouth of the gun feels like a chimney inside the painting, letting out invisible puffs of psychological smoke. All the exquisite details—the man’s glowing salmon-pink tunic, the cherrylike tip of his headdress, the fluffy tassels—are an excuse for pure visual stimulation.
paragraphs.zh: 在我看来，这是一个严重的误判。我们要怎样解释《巴希巴祖克》(1868—69)的美感？画面中的主体不过是一个戴着艳丽头饰的人。巴希巴祖克是奥斯曼军中的一种令人生畏的准军事队伍，以“砸碎头骨”而闻名；然而这个坐姿者却几乎是平静的象征，而非暴力的象征。他几乎可以确定是热罗姆给衣着打扮后的一位平民。画上他肩上的步枪并不打算很快开火；如果说有什么意义，这支枪口更像是画中一处烟囱，正在排出无形的心理烟雾。所有精巧细节——男子泛着光泽的鲑鱼粉色短袍、樱桃般的头饰顶端、蓬松的流苏——都只是为纯粹的视觉刺激提供借口。

paragraphs.en: Contra Zola, Gérôme’s talents as a draftsman, combined with his slick finishes and his touches of veracity in costume and design, show how style was just as important to him as any subject. If the aim of a successful Orientalist painting is to create a vehicle for escape, so that a textile manufacturer in France, say, can float out of his grimy industrial existence and swim into a golden dream of beauty or sex or violence, then the artist’s handling of paint has to be highly persuasive. Otherwise, the fantasy gets stuck on the canvas.
paragraphs.zh: 与左拉不同，热罗姆（Gérôme）的素描功底、光滑的表面处理，以及在服装和造型上的逼真点缀，表明风格对他而言与题材一样重要。若一幅成功的东方主义绘画的目标是成为逃逸之舟，让法国的一位纺织厂主例如能够从自己肮脏的工业生活中抽身，沉入关于美、性或暴力的黄金般梦境，那么画家的笔触处理就必须极具说服力；否则，幻想会停滞在画布之上。

paragraphs.en: At the end, the exhibition swings toward Hamdi. The curators present him as the more “authentic” Orientalist, less prone to stereotypes; instead of resorting to peepshows of exotic nudes, he faithfully documents mosques, Muslim scholars, dervishes, and quotidian scenes of the Ottoman capital. His most electric piece here is “At the Mosque Door” (1891). Approximately the size of a dining-room table, the canvas has fifteen people distributed along a set of stairs in front of a mosque, all of them flickering with very different kinds of life. A scrawny boy cracks a grin, a few women glide up the steps in silk robes, a man on the right rolls his left sleeve, another takes a nap, and the most impressive of them all, in a mustard-colored robe, shoots us a stern gaze.
paragraphs.zh: 最终，展览的重心转向哈姆迪（Hamdi）。策展人将他呈现为更“真实”的东方主义者，也就是较少落入刻板印象之中；他并未借助异域裸妇来博取猎奇，而是忠实地记录清真寺、穆斯林学者、苏菲教团教士以及奥斯曼首都的日常场景。他在此最有电光感的一件作品是《清真寺门前》（1891）。这幅画幅大约相当于一张餐桌的大小，画面中有十五个人物沿着清真寺前的一段台阶分布，他们各自闪现着迥异的生命状态。一个瘦小男孩露出顽皮的笑，一个或几位女子穿着丝绸长袍轻盈而上，一名男子在右侧卷起左袖，另一个人正在小憩，而其中最引人注目的是那位穿着芥末色长袍的人，他向我们投来一记严厉的目光。

paragraphs.en: Hamdi trained with Gustave Boulanger, a colleague of Gérôme’s, and although the painting has the crisp lineation of the academic-realist mode, it also has the fresh pulse of Impressionism, which Hamdi began to absorb in Paris in the eighteen-sixties. You can see it in the relaxed brushwork of the silk dresses and in the even coat of sunlight over everything. There’s none of the sultriness of contrast played up by Gérôme for mystique, and also no timeless air. Every detail is quickened by a momentary fizz, perfectly articulated by two pigeons about to pierce the picture plane at center. There’s also a nice little postmodern quirk: at least three of the men depicted are self-portraits of Hamdi.
paragraphs.zh: 哈姆迪在古斯塔夫·布兰热（Gustave Boulanger）门下受训，而布兰热是热罗姆的同僚。尽管这幅画具有学院派现实主义式的清晰勾勒，它同样带着哈姆迪在1860年代于巴黎开始吸收的印象主义的新鲜脉动。你可以在丝绸裙装中那种随意的笔法，以及遍及诸景的均匀日光色调里看到这一点。画面中没有热罗姆为了营造神秘感而故意加重的暧昧对比，也没有那种超脱时间的空灵气息。每一处细节都被一瞬间的起伏所点亮，而中央两只即将“穿透”画面平面的鸽子把这种感受表达得恰到好处。这里还有一个有趣的小后现代转折：画中至少有三位男性是哈姆迪自己的自画像。

paragraphs.en: Unfortunately, that’s Hamdi at his best. Much of his other work can be cloying or dull. “Dervish at the Children’s Tomb” (1903) is a heavy-handed portrayal of grief, and “Young Woman Reading” (1880), while theoretically interesting for its use of the spatial compression of Ottoman manuscript painting, doesn’t get beyond polite stasis. In general, Hamdi’s work has all the stage-managed feel of Gérôme’s, except with the heat of colonial lust plunged into an ice bath of poise. The exhibition’s framing of Hamdi as the conscientious Orientalist feels slyly designed to disarm judgment of his work, and even to neutralize his professional misdeeds. Hamdi was a powerful arts administrator who helped to approve excavation permits for foreign archeologists working in the Ottoman Empire, and what the exhibition alludes to as a “complex web of diplomatic exchanges” seems to be, quite simply, graft. Foreign art institutions and government officials, particularly in France, Germany, and the U.S., purchased Hamdi’s paintings and gave him awards in exchange for permits, and Hamdi, who wanted to succeed in Europe, seemed to appreciate the flattery. If his version of Orientalism entails paintings for permits, at the expense of cultural patrimony, does it really serve to benefit those whom it represents?
paragraphs.zh: 遗憾的是，这正是哈姆迪的高光时刻；他其他不少作品则令人腻烦或平淡。《儿童墓前的苦行者》（1903）对悲痛的呈现显得生硬，而《年轻女子读书》（1880）虽然从奥斯曼手稿绘画的空间压缩手法上看很有理论趣味，却止步于客套般的停滞。总体而言，哈姆迪的作品与热罗姆的作品一样，带着明显的舞台化痕迹，只不过把殖民欲望的炽热浸入了姿态沉稳的冷水之中。展览将哈姆迪塑造成“负责任的东方主义者”的叙事，似乎是有意巧妙地化解对其作品的评判，甚至淡化他职业行为上的失当。哈姆迪是位颇有权势的艺术行政官员，曾帮助批准外国考古学家在奥斯曼帝国境内的发掘许可；展陈所提到的“复杂的外交交流网络”似乎只是委婉说法，实则就是寻租。外国艺术机构和政府官员，尤其是法国、德国与美国方面，购买了哈姆迪的画作并授予他奖项，以换取这些许可。哈姆迪本人希望在欧洲站稳脚跟，因此似乎乐于接受这种奉承。若他的东方主义版本建立在“换取许可的绘画”之上，以文化遗产为代价，那么它真的能为那些被其描绘的人群带来益处吗？

paragraphs.en: To funnel the exhibition down to Hamdi and a group of mostly French artists, Gérôme above all, makes the project of Orientalism feel oddly bite-size. Although the curators have broadened the scope beyond paintings, many of the objects are here to corroborate what’s already in the paintings—hammam shoes and rifles in Gérôme, a wooden chest and gold ear ornaments in Delacroix, parasols in Hamdi—and, despite the impressive research and archival sleuthing, the result is a sort of empty forensic effect, like evidence being presented in a trial for nothing. The show is certainly worth seeing, though. There’s something remarkable about Orientalism, the way it stuns the mind and makes it impervious to nuance. Is there any other bad idea that has produced so many beautiful things? ♦
paragraphs.zh: 当展览被缩窄到 Hamdi 及一群主要为法国艺术家的作品，尤其是 Gérôme 时，东方主义这一议题便显得有些“咬一口就够”了。尽管策展人已将范围扩展到绘画之外，但展出的许多实物似乎只是用来佐证画作中已有内容——Gérôme 的哈马姆拖鞋和步枪、Delacroix 的木箱和金耳饰、Hamdi 的伞——尽管考据和档案调查十分出色，最终却带来一种空泛的“法证式”效果，仿佛在法庭上展示证据却没有案件可判。尽管如此，这场展览值得一看。东方主义令人惊叹的一点，是它如何震撼人的心智，让人难以捕捉其细微差别。还有什么别的“坏主意”能产出如此之多的美丽事物吗？♦
